title: Sabut Moong Dal

overview.description: Whole green moong dal pressure-cooked with a deeply flavoured onion, tomato and spice masala, then finished with a fragrant tempering.

ingredients: 1 tbsp lemon juice, or to taste
method: Rinse the whole green moong dal thoroughly.
For the best texture, soak the dal in plenty of water for at least 4 hours, or overnight.
Drain the dal before cooking.
Set the multi-cooker to sauté mode and add the oil and ghee.
Add the cumin seeds and cook until fragrant.
Add the onion and cook until softened and lightly golden.
Add the garlic, ginger and green chilli if using.
Cook for 1–2 minutes.
Add the chopped tomatoes, ground coriander, ground cumin, turmeric, Kashmiri chilli powder and salt.
Cook until the tomatoes have softened and the masala has thickened.
Add the drained moong dal and stir to coat it in the masala.
Add the water and stir well, scraping the bottom of the pot to remove any stuck masala.
Cancel sauté mode and secure the lid.
Pressure cook on high pressure for 18 minutes.
Allow the pressure to release naturally for at least 15 minutes before releasing any remaining pressure.
Open the lid and stir the dal.
If the dal is thinner than desired, use sauté mode to simmer it uncovered until thickened.
Stir in the garam masala and lemon juice.
For the finishing tadka, heat the ghee in a small pan.
Add the cumin seeds.
When they begin to sizzle, add the sliced garlic and dried chilli.
Cook until the garlic is lightly golden.
Pour the hot tadka over the dal.
Finish with fresh coriander and serve.

learning.did-you-know: Whole green moong beans are the same pulse as moong dal, but with the green outer skin left intact. This gives them a firmer texture and a more pronounced, nutty flavour.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How soaking and pressure cooking transform a whole pulse, how to build a deeply flavoured masala in a multi-cooker and how a final tadka adds a fresh layer of flavour.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A more substantial dal that demonstrates how the same pulse can have a completely different character when cooked whole rather than split and skinned.
chapman-tips: Soaking the whole moong is strongly recommended for a more even texture and shorter pressure-cooking time.
The dal should be tender but still retain some of its shape.
Don't rush the onion and tomato masala; this is where much of the flavour develops.
Scrape the bottom of the multi-cooker thoroughly before pressure cooking to reduce the risk of a burn warning.
The dal will thicken considerably as it rests.
The finishing tadka is optional but gives the dish a much more pronounced final layer of flavour.
For a milder family version, leave the green chilli whole or omit it while keeping the Kashmiri chilli powder for colour and flavour.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Cool completely before freezing in airtight portions. Thaw overnight in the refrigerator and reheat gently, adding water as necessary to loosen the dal.
notes.leftovers: Thin with water and reheat as a soup, serve with rice or use as a filling for chapati or paratha.
